title: Lesson 41 — The Detective

words.definition: based on inference rather than direct observation; incidental
words.example: The case rested on circumstantial evidence alone.

words.definition: not able to be denied or disputed
serving to settle an issue decisively; convincing
not leading to a firm conclusion; not decisive
words.example: The test results were frustratingly inconclusive.

words.definition: confirming or supporting a statement or finding
words.example: Two corroborating witnesses backed her account.

words.definition: tending to clear someone of guilt or blame
words.example: The lawyer withheld the exculpatory footage until trial.

words.definition: making someone appear guilty of a crime or wrongdoing
words.example: Investigators found incriminating messages on his phone.

words.definition: a formal statement given as evidence, especially in court
words.example: Her testimony contradicted the defendant's account.

words.definition: the reaching of a conclusion by reasoning from known facts
words.example: By careful deduction, he identified the culprit.

words.definition: a person responsible for a crime or wrongdoing
words.example: The culprit had left a single glove behind.

words.definition: to formally declare someone not guilty of a crime
words.example: The jury voted to acquit after two hours.

story: The first evidence against him was only [[circumstantial|circumstantial]] — nothing [[incontrovertible|incontrovertible]], nothing [[irrefutable|irrefutable]], nothing [[conclusive|conclusive]] enough to charge him. The lab tests came back [[inconclusive|inconclusive]] too. Then two [[corroborating|corroborating]] witnesses came forward, and a set of oddly [[exculpatory|exculpatory]] texts turned out, on a second reading, to be quietly [[incriminating|incriminating]]. Their [[testimony|testimony]], paired with careful [[deduction|deduction]], finally pointed to the real [[culprit|culprit]] — and let the original suspect be [[acquit|acquitted]] at last.
